claimed_contribution: The paper identifies a new form of memory blocking, metacognitive memory blocking, in which feeling-of-not-knowing blocks use of implicit memory during judgment.
It is the first to show that attempting to recall information in explicit form can disrupt use of the same information in implicit form because of metacognitive experience from recall failure.
It provides further evidence for dual representations of price memory and argues that many memory-based price comparisons are made via implicit memory rather than explicit recall.
context: The studies are set in memory-based grocery price judgments, where participants compare current prices with previously seen prices for common products. Data come from U.S. MTurk participants and a university lab student sample.
design_and_data: Across six experiments, participants first completed an encoding task viewing 20 grocery products with prices and making purchase decisions, without warning about a later memory test. They then completed an unexpected price-comparison task with updated prices (+/-$1), typically in compare-only versus recall-and-compare conditions; recall-and-compare participants were asked to attempt recall before each comparison but could skip if unable. Process tests added an unrelated name-recall condition, an attention-to-feeling-of-not-knowing manipulation, and construal-level manipulations (primed how/why mindset and measured chronic BIF). Analyses used mixed-model repeated-measures logistic regression (PROC GLIMMIX), plus a raw-data meta-analysis across six studies (N = 903).
main_findings: Participants asked to attempt price recall before comparing prices were less accurate than compare-only participants when recall was difficult; a meta-analysis showed 57.6% versus 50.7% accuracy (Cohen's d = .46).
Attempting recall increased indicators of feeling-of-not-knowing (for example, more 'don't know' responses), and feeling-of-not-knowing mediated the recall effect under control conditions.

The blocking effect depended on metacognitive attribution and attention: failing to recall unrelated names did not produce the same comparison impairment, drawing attention to not-knowing reduced compare-only accuracy, and abstract construal attenuated recall-induced impairment despite similar recall-failure rates.
one_line_summary: Across six experiments on grocery prices, attempting and failing to explicitly recall past prices reduced subsequent comparison accuracy by inducing a feeling-of-not-knowing that temporarily blocks reliance on implicit price memory.
relation_to_prior_work: extends prior memory-blocking research by showing a mechanism based on metacognitive feeling-of-not-knowing rather than memory-content change or retrieval competition, and extends remember-versus-know price-memory work by showing that explicit recall attempts can impair judgments that could otherwise use implicit memory.
research_gap: Prior consumer price-memory studies and common intuition generally assume that asking for recall before comparison is helpful or at least harmless, and classic blocking accounts focus on memory-content or accessibility changes. The paper addresses the untested possibility that recall failure itself can create a task-relevant feeling-of-not-knowing that blocks use of intact implicit memory in subsequent comparisons.
research_question: Does attempting explicit recall of prior prices before making price comparisons improve or impair the accuracy of memory-based comparisons? If it impairs accuracy, is the mechanism a metacognitive feeling-of-not-knowing that blocks reliance on implicit memory, and can this effect be moderated by relevance and attentional focus?
